name: api-fuzzing-for-bug-bounty
description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "test API security", "fuzz APIs", "find IDOR vulnerabilities", "test REST API", "test GraphQL", "API penetration testing", "bug bounty API testing", or needs guidance on API security assessment techniques.
qualityReason: Detailed and focused on bug hunting
capabilities: Guide systematic API reconnaissance and endpoint discovery using tools like Kiterunner and Swagger/OpenAPI analysis
Provide IDOR testing techniques including parameter manipulation, array wrapping, and wildcard injection methods
Demonstrate injection testing for SQL, command, XXE, and SSRF vulnerabilities in API contexts
Explain GraphQL-specific attacks including introspection queries, batching bypasses, and nested query DoS
Document authentication bypass techniques and rate limit testing strategies across API versions
Reference industry-standard tools like InQL, GraphCrawler, and APIKit for automated security testing
author: zebbern
securitySummary: This skill is a markdown documentation file containing educational content about API security testing methodologies. The static analyzer flagged 75 patterns (external commands, network URLs, injection payloads), but all are false positives - they exist within markdown code blocks as instructional examples, not executable code. The content covers legitimate bug bounty techniques including IDOR testing, SQL injection detection, GraphQL security, and authentication bypass methods. Suitable for publication as educational security content for authorized testing engagements.
valueStatement: API vulnerabilities are among the most common and critical findings in bug bounty programs. This skill provides comprehensive techniques for testing REST, GraphQL, and SOAP APIs to discover IDOR, injection, and authentication bypass vulnerabilities.
userTitle: Master API Security Testing for Bug Bounty
limitations: This skill provides educational guidance only - actual testing requires user-operated tools like Burp Suite
Techniques must only be used on systems where you have explicit authorization (bug bounty programs, owned systems)
Does not replace professional penetration testing expertise or automated scanning tools
Examples are illustrative - real-world API security testing requires adaptation to specific targets

useCases.targetUser: Bug bounty hunters and independent security researchers
useCases.title: Bug Bounty Hunter API Testing
useCases.description: Systematic approach to discovering API vulnerabilities in authorized bug bounty programs, focusing on high-impact findings like IDOR and authentication bypasses.

useCases.targetUser: Professional penetration testers and security consultants
useCases.title: Penetration Testing Engagement Support
useCases.description: Comprehensive API security testing methodology for professional penetration testers conducting authorized assessments of client APIs.

useCases.title: Developer Security Training
useCases.description: Educational resource for developers to understand API attack vectors and implement appropriate security controls in their applications.

promptTemplates.title: Basic API Reconnaissance
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to test the security of an API endpoint at https://target.com/api/v1. Help me identify potential attack surfaces and create a testing checklist for common API vulnerabilities.

promptTemplates.title: IDOR Testing Methodology
promptTemplates.prompt: I found an API endpoint /api/v1/users/{id} that returns user data. Guide me through systematic IDOR testing techniques including parameter manipulation, array injection, and bypass methods.
promptTemplates.scenario: Intermediate - Testing for broken object-level authorization

promptTemplates.title: GraphQL Security Assessment
promptTemplates.prompt: The target has a GraphQL endpoint at /graphql with introspection enabled. Help me analyze the schema for security issues and test for common GraphQL vulnerabilities including batching attacks and authorization bypasses.

promptTemplates.title: API Injection Testing
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm testing a REST API that accepts JSON input. Provide a comprehensive injection testing strategy covering SQL injection in JSON fields, command injection via parameters, and XXE payloads for XML endpoints.

bestPractices: Always obtain explicit written authorization before testing any API - only test systems you own or have permission to assess through bug bounty programs
Test all API versions (v1, v2, v3) separately as security controls often differ between versions
Document all findings with clear reproduction steps, HTTP requests, and impact analysis for responsible disclosure
antiPatterns: Never assume mobile, web, and developer APIs have identical security controls - test each surface independently
Do not skip undocumented endpoints - hidden admin and debug endpoints often have weaker security
Avoid testing without rate limit awareness - implement delays and consider using batched requests responsibly

faq.question: Is API fuzzing legal for bug bounty hunting?
faq.answer: Yes, when performed on systems with explicit authorization through bug bounty programs or on systems you own. Always read and follow the specific program's rules of engagement and scope definitions.

faq.question: What tools do I need to perform API security testing?
faq.answer: Essential tools include Burp Suite or similar proxy, API wordlists like SecLists, and specialized tools like Kiterunner for discovery, InQL for GraphQL, and custom Python scripts for automation.

faq.question: What is the most common API vulnerability?
faq.answer: IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference), also known as BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization), is consistently among the most common and impactful API vulnerabilities in bug bounty programs.

faq.question: How do I test GraphQL APIs differently from REST?
faq.answer: GraphQL requires testing introspection queries, batching attacks for rate limit bypass, nested query DoS, and schema-based authorization analysis. The single-endpoint architecture changes the attack surface significantly.

faq.question: What should I do if I find a critical API vulnerability?
faq.answer: Document the vulnerability with clear reproduction steps, minimize data access during testing, and submit a responsible disclosure report through the appropriate channel (bug bounty platform or security contact).

faq.question: Can this skill help me learn API security from scratch?
faq.answer: This skill provides comprehensive techniques but assumes foundational knowledge of HTTP, web security concepts, and basic penetration testing methodology. Beginners should start with OWASP API Security Top 10 documentation.
